titleStmt.title: Letter from William Thompson to the President & Fellows of the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland, 30 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Henry Thompson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from William Henry Thompson (1861-1929) to the President & Fellows of the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland (RCPI) regarding Thompson's choice to volunteer as a temporary assistant in lunatic asylums in England and Scotland. Thompson volunteers himself in the hope that his offer will relieve the number of asylum vacancies due to the war or free men of military age to sign up.

msIdentifier.repository: Royal College of Physicians of Ireland

correspAction.placeName: Royal College of Physicians, Kildare Street, Dublin, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
School of Physiology
Trinity College. 30th May 1916 Dear Sirs, Having learned that great
difficulty is experienced in finding
temporary assistants in Lunatic Asylums
in England & Scotland, to take the place
of the younger medical men who are
called up for military service, I volun-
teered, if granted leave of absence, to do
such work, if by doing so it would relieve
a man to join the R.A.M.C. A plan was
drawn up and submitted to the Board
of Trinity College by which, conjointly with
Dr. Lamb, who is also over military age.
The work of the Department here as well
as that of an assistancy at an Asylum
in England or Scotland, could be carried out.
(Briefly the suggestion was that Dr. Lamb
could take charge of the work of the first
or Michaelmas Term and also of the Trinity
or Summer Term here. That of the Hilary
Term, or January to March (inclusive) to be done
by myself. During this term Dr. Lamb would
relieve me in the Asylum work. By this
arrangement the heavy term including
the March examinations, would be in my
own hands. A condition was also to be made
that I should be given permission to return
for the June and October examinations.
the Board has agreed to this arrangement
and has granted me leave of absence
for the duration of the war.) Within a month, no less than four
requests reached me to take up Asylum
Assistance work and I have accepted
and been appointed to act as Temporary
Assistant Physician and Pathologist
At the Crichton Royal Institution Dum
fries, to take up work on 1st July next. I therefore beg to ask the President and
Fellows of teh Royal College of Physicians
to satisfy the permission granted to me
by the Board of Trinity College. I should
add that I hold a Diploma in Mental Diseases
and in my younger days did Asylum
work. I may also state that the authorities
at Dumfries are agreeable to give me
permission to carry out the duties of the
examinerships I hold, other than those
in the School of Physic. I am
Your obed. servt W.H. Thompson To The President & Fellows of the
Royal College of Physicians in
Ireland.
